text.title: Prometheus: Recording Rules, Alert Quality, and Alertmanager Routing
text.summary: Rules for naming recording rules, writing high-quality alert rules, and routing alerts via Alertmanager.

rule.statement: Name recording rules using the pattern `level:metric:operations` (e.g., `service:request_rate:5m`).
rule.rationale: The naming convention identifies the aggregation level, the base metric, and the operations applied, making rules self-documenting and discoverable in PromQL autocomplete.

rule.statement: Create recording rules for any PromQL expression used in more than one dashboard panel or alert.
rule.rationale: Pre-computing expensive aggregations with recording rules reduces query latency from seconds to milliseconds for dashboard loading. Prometheus evaluates recording rules on a schedule, not per query.

rule.statement: Every alert must include a `for` clause of at least 5 minutes; use 10–15 minutes for infrastructure alerts.
rule.rationale: Without `for`, a single spike triggers the alert. With `for: 5m`, the condition must persist for 5 minutes before firing, eliminating most transient false positives.

rule.statement: Every alert must include `runbook_url` in annotations linking to the remediation procedure.
rule.rationale: On-call engineers receiving an alert at 3am need immediate context. An alert without a runbook link forces them to search before they can act, increasing MTTR.

rule.statement: Alert on symptoms (user-facing impact) not causes (CPU high). Prefer error rate and latency alerts over resource saturation alerts.
rule.rationale: High CPU does not mean users are affected. A high error rate always means users are affected. Symptom-based alerts have fewer false positives and clearer remediation paths.

rule.statement: Route `severity: critical` alerts to PagerDuty and Slack; `severity: warning` to Slack only; use inhibition rules to suppress warnings when critical is firing for the same alertname + namespace.
rule.rationale: Without inhibition, a single outage generates dozens of redundant warning alerts alongside the critical. Inhibition reduces noise so on-call engineers focus on the critical root cause.

antipattern.description: Alert without `for` clause on a rate() expression.
antipattern.reason: rate() over short windows is noisy. Without `for`, every brief spike fires and resolves the alert, causing alert fatigue where engineers stop responding.

text.reference: PrometheusRule template with recording rules and alerts
